coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped coal-based carbon quantum dots enhance the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution of Co-Fe-P derived from MOF
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Shiyong
Wang, Yijun
Wu, Yue
Li, Mei
coredata.dc:description: In order to explore the functional application of coal and realize the high-value application of low-quality coal. Therefore, low-cost coal as a carbon source into environmental protection, high-value coal-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs). This study employed high-sulfur coal as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen source to successfully synthesize nitrogen and sulfur co-doped coal-based carbon quantum dots (NSCQDs) via a one-pot hydrothermal method. These NSCQDs were utilized as co-catalysts to assist the photocatalytic water splitting and hydrogen evolution of metal phosphides (Co-P and Fe-P). The obtained catalysts were subjected to systematic structural and property analyses, and the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity and stability were investigated, along with an analysis of the photocatalytic mechanism. The results revealed that the novel composite photocatalyst exhibited high hydrogen evolution activity, with a maximum hydrogen evolution rate of 28.41 mmol·g−1·h−1 at pH 10. Under a wavelength of 520 nm, the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) reached 4.93%, and the solar-to-hydrogen (STH) conversion efficiency reached 0.46%. The semiconductor type (n-type) of the monophasic catalyst was determined through Mott-Schottky analysis, and the band structure was analyzed accordingly. The conduction and valence band positions for Co-P were determined to be -0.44 eV and 1.31 eV, respectively, while those for Fe-P were found to be -0.51 eV and 1.17 eV, respectively. Based on these results, it is inferred that an S-type heterojunction is formed between Co-P and Fe-P, and NSCQDs enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution as an auxiliary catalyst. This research advances the synthesis of coal-based carbon quantum dots and provides the key information for the photocatalytic decomposition of water and hydrogen evolution using coal-based CQDs.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped coal-based carbon quantum dots enhance the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution of Co-Fe-P derived from MOF

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to explore the functional application of coal and realize the high-value application of low-quality coal. Therefore, low-cost coal as a carbon source into environmental protection, high-value coal-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs). This study employed high-sulfur coal as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen source to successfully synthesize nitrogen and sulfur co-doped coal-based carbon quantum dots (NSCQDs) via a one-pot hydrothermal method. These NSCQDs were utilized as co-catalysts to assist the photocatalytic water splitting and hydrogen evolution of metal phosphides (Co-P and Fe-P). The obtained catalysts were subjected to systematic structural and property analyses, and the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity and stability were investigated, along with an analysis of the photocatalytic mechanism. The results revealed that the novel composite photocatalyst exhibited high hydrogen evolution activity, with a maximum hydrogen evolution rate of 28.41 mmol·g−1·h−1 at pH 10. Under a wavelength of 520 nm, the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) reached 4.93%, and the solar-to-hydrogen (STH) conversion efficiency reached 0.46%. The semiconductor type (n-type) of the monophasic catalyst was determined through Mott-Schottky analysis, and the band structure was analyzed accordingly. The conduction and valence band positions for Co-P were determined to be -0.44 eV and 1.31 eV, respectively, while those for Fe-P were found to be -0.51 eV and 1.17 eV, respectively. Based on these results, it is inferred that an S-type heterojunction is formed between Co-P and Fe-P, and NSCQDs enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution as an auxiliary catalyst. This research advances the synthesis of coal-based carbon quantum dots and provides the key information for the photocatalytic decomposition of water and hydrogen evolution using coal-based CQDs.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing consumption of non-renewable energy sources such as coal and petroleum, environmental pollution issues have become increasingly prominent [1–3]. The search for clean energy has become an urgent priority [4]. Hydrogen gas, known for its high energy density, zero emissions, and nontoxic combustion process, has been recognized as a clean energy source [5,6]. Nowadays, numerous scholars have been involved in the research of clean energy, for hydrogen, some use electrocatalysis to produce hydrogen, some use photocatalysis to produce hydrogen. Photocatalytic hydrogen production utilizes solar energy and visible light irradiation in the presence of catalysts to achieve water splitting and hydrogen evolution [7]. Compared with electrocatalytic hydrogen production, photocatalytic hydrogen production is more environmentally friendly, more energy-saving, is an ideal method for hydrogen production [8,9]. Because solar energy is renewable energy, with low cost, environmental protection, clean and other characteristics [10,11]. Throughout the research process, various efficient photocatalysts have been developed. However, some existing photocatalysts still suffer from limitations, such as weak electron-hole separation ability and rapid recombination, resulting in limited photocatalytic hydrogen production capabilities [12,13]. Moreover, some materials exhibit issues related to instability, easily decomposed, and high overpotentials during the reaction. Therefore, the development of stable and high-performance catalysts is essential.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a class of novel materials that can be synthesized by selecting different metal ions as the central metal centers and utilizing organic compounds as frameworks. MOFs offer advantages such as simple preparation methods, large surface areas, and abundant reaction sites, which make them widely used in catalysis research [14]. Due to the unique structure and composition of MOF materials, they are well-suited as precursors and provide a platform for designing high-performance catalysts [15,16]. Phosphorus atoms in phosphides have high electronegativity and can capture positively charged protons (H +), thereby modulating the electron density on the catalyst surface and enhancing hydrogen evolution activity [17]. Li et al. [18] synthesized a bimetallic CeCo-MOF via a hydrothermal method, and by adjusting the ratio of Ce and Co sources during phosphidation, achieved the highest hydrogen evolution rate of 8040.9 μmol·g−1·h−1 at a Ce-to-Co ratio of 1:2. Jiang et al. [19] prepared a composite catalyst Ni-MOF-P(Ni2P)/In2O3 by calcination-phosphidation of Ni-MOF-74 and In2O3. The optimal hydrogen evolution amount reached 494.17 μmol when the mass ratio of Ni2P to In2O3 was 1:0.3, which was three times higher than that of Ni-MOF-P. Li et al. [20] synthesized a Co3O4@CoP composite catalyst by combining Co3O4 and CoP derivatives of ZIF-67, achieving a high hydrogen evolution activity of 11,167 μmol·h−1·g−1 under optimal conditions. Kampouri et al. [21] reported a composite catalyst Ni2P/MIL-125-NH2 prepared by using MIL-125-NH2 and nickel phosphide (Ni2P) nanoparticles, which exhibited three times higher photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity than Pt/MIL-125-NH2, with a hydrogen evolution rate of 894 μmol·h−1·g−1. It is noteworthy that transition metal phosphides sometimes exhibit higher activity in hydrogen evolution than noble metal Pt. However, MOF materials also suffer from issues of poor stability and susceptibility to decomposition. To address this problem, this study aims to use low-temperature phosphidation to synthesize transition metal phosphides from MOF precursors, which can enhance the material's activity and stability.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a novel nanomaterial that has found wide applications in fields such as photocatalysis and electrocatalysis. CQDs can also be used in biocatalysis with the development of the research on the application of nanomaterials and biocatalysis [22–24]. Due to their small size, easy modification, rich surface functional groups, and the ability to incorporate different elements as doping agents, CQDs have gained significant attention from researchers in recent years. CQDs possess excellent optical properties, which can broaden the light absorption range of materials and improve visible light utilization. In terms of electrical properties, CQDs can act as electron reservoirs [25] and exhibit remarkable performance in electron transport and capture [26]. By enhancing the migration and separation efficiency of photoinduced charge carriers [27], CQDs can improve photocatalytic activity. Doping with different elements is an effective method to improve the performance of CQDs, with nitrogen (N) doping being the most common. Nitrogen-doped CQDs can effectively increase electron density and adjust optical properties [28], resulting in significant improvements in catalytic activity. Nitrogen doping in CQDs can make the electrons in the conduction band more active, thereby enhancing the performance of CQDs [29]. Chen et al. [25] synthesized nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon quantum dots (NPCQDs), and the hydrogen evolution activity of the resulting composite catalyst NPCQD/ZnO (NPCZ) reached 417 μmol·h−1·g−1. Xu et al. [30] prepared NCQDs via a hydrothermal method and employed them to enhance CdS photocatalytic hydrogen evolution, achieving a maximum rate of 2306.1 μmol·h−1·g−1. The catalytic activity of the composite catalyst involving NCQDs increased seven-fold compared to the control. However, sulfur doping is less common in CQDs due to the larger atomic radius of sulfur (S) than carbon and the smaller electronegativity difference between C and S, making it challenging to introduce sulfur as a dopant from external sources. Nevertheless, high-sulfur coal exhibits prominent natural conditions, enabling in situ sulfur doping of carbon quantum dots through its inherent sulfur content.

In this study, Co-MOF and Fe-MOF will be used as precursors to prepare the corresponding transition metal phosphides via phosphidation. High-sulfur lignite coal will serve as the carbon source, and a small amount of urea will be introduced as the nitrogen source. A green synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped coal-based carbon quantum dots will be achieved through a hydrothermal method. These nitrogen and sulfur co-doped coal-based carbon quantum dots will be employed as co-catalysts to investigate their enhancement effect on the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution of composite catalysts and elucidate the hydrogen evolution mechanism.

Two types of coal samples were used in the experiment: Inner Mongolian Tongliao lignite (TL) and Yunnan Lianhuatang lignite (LHT). The following reagents were used in the experiment and purchased from Aladdin as analytical grade: 2-methylimidazole (C4H6N2), Cobalt(II) nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), Ferric chloride (FeCl3), phthalic acid (C8H6O4), N,N-dimethylformamide (C3H7NO), Eosin Y (C20H6Br4Na2O5), Triethanolamine (C6H15NO3), and Anhydrous ethanol (CH3CH2OH).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proximate and ultimate analysis of coal samples and sulfur forms analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The coal samples were crushed and sieved to obtain particles with a size smaller than 74 μm. According to GB/T 212–2008, the proximate analysis of coal samples was conducted. The forms of sulfur in the coal samples were analyzed according to GB/T 214–2007. Table 1 shows the results of proximate and ultimate analysis of coal samples and sulfur forms analysis.

Preparation of Co-MOF: 1.30 g of 2-methylimidazole was weighed and dissolved in 8 mL of water, and 0.58 g of Co(NO3)2·6H2O was weighed and dissolved in 40 mL of water. After complete dissolution of both, the Co(NO3)2·6H2O solution was slowly added dropwise to the 2-methylimidazole solution with continuous stirring. The mixture was stirred for 4 h, aged overnight, filtered, washed, and dried to obtain the Co-MOF sample.

Preparation of Fe-MOF: 0.80 g of FeCl3 and 0.83 g of terephthalic acid were weighed and dissolved in 25 mL of N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF). The mixture was stirred for 1 h, and then 2 mL of 2 M NaOH solution was added dropwise. The reaction mixture was transferred to a reaction vessel and maintained at 100 °C for 12 h. After cooling, the sample was centrifuged, collected, and placed in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h to dry. The collected sample is referred to as Fe-MOF.

Sodium hypophosphite (The mass of sodium hypophosphite is 3 times that of MOF) was weighed in proportion and thoroughly ground with Co-MOF or Fe-MOF. The mixture was transferred to an alumina boat and placed in a tube furnace for phosphidation under nitrogen protection. The temperature in the furnace was set to 300 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C/min. After reaching the set temperature, the mixture was maintained for 2 h. After natural cooling, the sample was collected, ground, transferred to a beaker, and mixed with a certain amount of deionized water. The mixture was stirred on a magnetic stirrer for 10 min, filtered, and washed multiple times with deionized water during filtration to remove impurities. After washing, the solid sample was collected and placed in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h. The phosphidated Co-MOF and Fe-MOF were named Co-P and Fe-P, respectivel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of coal-based carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: 2.00 g of coal powder and 60 mL of distilled water were transferred into a high-pressure reaction vessel. The mixture was kept at 180 °C for 10 h. After the hydrothermal reaction, the reaction vessel was allowed to cool to room temperature, and the contents were filtered and purified by dialysis. The resulting TL and LHT carbon quantum dot powders were obtained by freeze-drying at −40 °C and were labeled as CQDs and SCQDs, respectively. Additionally, 2.00 g of coal powder and 0.02 g of urea were subjected to the same preparation conditions as CQDs to obtain TL and LHT nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots, labeled as NCQDs and NSCQDs, respectively.
The specific preparation processes for each catalyst are illustrated in Fig. 1 , the catalyst preparation flowchart.

The Co-MOF, Fe-MOF, NSCQDs, and 15 mL of anhydrous ethanol were proportionally combined and placed in a 50 mL glass beaker. The mixture was sonicated for 10 min and then transferred to a water bath. While stirring, the excess anhydrous ethanol was evaporated at 80 °C until dry. The dried sample was collected and placed in an oven for further drying. The dried mixture and sodium hypophosphite (mass of sodium hypophosphite is three times that of the dried mixture) were weighed proportionally, ground together, and then transferred into an alumina crucible. The crucible was placed into a tube furnace for phosphidation. The conditions for the tube furnace were the same as those used for the preparation of Co-P and Fe-P. The subsequent steps were performed as described earlier. The resulting phosphidated composite sample was named Co-Fe-Px/y%NSCQDs (where x represents the mass ratio of Co-MOF to Fe-MOF, and y% represents the percentage of NSCQDs relative to the total mass of Co-MOF and Fe-MOF).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed using a Rigaku Smartlab diffractometer (Rigaku RINT-2000) from Japan. The changes in the specific surface area and pore structure of the samples were characterized using a Micromeritics ASAP 2460 surface area and porosity analyzer (BET) at 150 °C under vacuum for 6 h. The instrument operated at a working current of 40 mA and a working voltage of 40 kV. Morphological observations were conducted using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI Talos F200S) operating at 200 kV. Some morphological characterizations were carried out using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) (ZEISS Sigma 300). Prior to imaging, a Quorum SC7620 sputter coater was used to apply a gold coating for 45 s, and the acceleration voltage during imaging was set at 3 kV. The elemental composition and chemical states of the catalysts were characterized using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (Thermo Fisher ESCALAB Xi+). Steady-state and transient fluorescence tests were performed using a steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (FluoroMax-4) from Horiba Scientific. The zeta potential measurements were conducted using a Zeta potential analyzer (LITESIZER 500).

The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution experiments were conducted in 65 mL quartz bottles. In each quartz reaction bottle, 10 mg of the self-prepared catalyst and 20 mg of Eosin dye were weighed and then 30 mL of a 10 % TEOA (triethanolamine) aqueous solution was added. After 10 min of ultrasonication, the bottles were purged with N2 for 10 min to remove impurities from the quartz bottle. The experiments were carried out in a multi-channel photochemical reaction apparatus equipped with a 5 W LED (λ≥420 nm) light source. During the reaction, magnetic stirring was employed to ensure the homogeneous dispersion of the catalyst in the aqueous solution. The reaction lasted for 5 h, with sampling taken every 1 h and 0.5 mL of gas extracted. The hydrogen gas production was determined using a gas chromatograph (Tianmei GC7900, TCD, 13X column, with N2 as the carrier gas). Five cyclic stability tests were conducted under the same conditions. After each cycle, the gas in the hydrogen production bottle was replaced to maintain an anaerobic system. The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) test was performed using band-pass filters at different wavelengths (420, 475, 520, 550, and 600 nm) with a 300 W xenon lamp as light source. The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) was measured at different wavelengths.

The photoelectrochemical properties of different catalysts were measured using a CHI660E electrochemical workstation. The working electrode was subjected to transient photocurrent response (I-T), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), alternating current impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and Mott-Schottky (MS) analysis. In the standard three-electrode cell, the photoelectrochemical tests were conducted using a 0.2 mol·L−1 Na2SO4 solution as the electrolyte, and platinum plate as auxiliary electrode and Hg/HgO as reference electrode, the working electrode is ITO conductive glass coated with mixed solution of photocatalyst. Using 300 W xenon lamp as irradiation source, the effective area of working electrode is 1 × 1 cm2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological structure characterization and composition analysis

ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the crystal structure of the catalyst, XRD characterization was performed on the prepared catalysts. Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of the samples, where Fig. 2(a) represents the XRD patterns of CoP and Fe2P. It was observed that the prepared CoP exhibited diffraction peaks at 31.609°, 35.336°, 36.333°, 46.209°, 48.132°, 52.174°, 56.083°, and 56.714°, corresponding to the (011), (200), (111), (112), (211), (103), (020), and (013) crystal planes, respectively, as indicated by the standard card (PDF#65–2593). The XRD spectrum of Fe2P showed diffraction peaks at 31.279°, 40.301°, 44.221°, 47.311°, 52.944°, and 54.124°, corresponding to the (101), (111), (201), (210), (002), and (300) crystal planes, respectively, as indicated by the standard card (PDF#69–1900). This indicates that the materials obtained after low-temperature phosphorization of Co-MOF and Fe-MOF are CoP and Fe2P, respectively. Fig. 2(b) shows the XRD results of composite catalysts with different ratios. It can be observed that the composite catalysts exhibit characteristic peaks corresponding to both CoP and Fe2P, indicating that the composite catalysts mainly consist of CoP and Fe2P components.

The microstructures of the catalyst were studied using SEM and TEM. Fig. 3 shows the SEM and TEM images of the different samples. In Fig. 3(a), the SEM image of Co-MOF reveals a leaf-like structure with smooth large planes. Fig. 3(b) shows the SEM image of Fe-MOF, which exhibits a 3D polyhedral structure. Figs. 3(c) and (d) present the SEM images of Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs. From Fig. 3(c), it can be observed that the phosphorized Co-Fe-P sample exhibits a nano-sheet-like structure with a layered and stacked morphology. This morphology is attributed to the fragmentation of the Co-MOF and Fe-MOF structures during phosphorization, resulting in the formation of smaller sheet-like structures. Further analysis with Fig. 3(d) reveals the presence of pores of varying sizes on the surface of the phosphorized sample. The formation of these pores can increase the specific surface area, facilitate electron transfer, provide more reaction channels for free electrons, and offer more active sites for catalytic reactions. In Fig. 3(e), the TEM image of CQDs (inserted graph shows the particle size distribution of CQDs) is presented. Particle size analysis using the Nano Measure software reveals a maximum particle size of 4.11 nm, a minimum particle size of 1.27 nm, and an average particle size of 2.43 nm. Similarly, in Fig. 3(f), the TEM image of NSCQDs (inserted graph shows the particle size distribution of NSCQDs) shows a maximum particle size of 5.73 nm, a minimum particle size of 1.19 nm, and an average particle size of 3.26 nm.

By comparing the particle sizes of CQDs and NSCQDs, it is observed that nitrogen and sulfur doping can increase the particle size of carbon quantum dots. Figure S1. (a) and (b) show the fluorescence test results of CQDs and NSCQDs at different temperatures. It shows that CQDs and NSCQDs have good stability and can adapt to different temperature conditions. Figure S1. (c) and (d) show the fluorescence test results of CQDs and NSCQDs before and after UV lamp irradiation. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs and NSCQDs did not decrease after 60 min of UV irradiation and remained unchanged. It can be seen that CQDs has fluorescence stability under long-time ultraviolet light. The calculated quantum yields of CQDs and NSCQDs are 0.81 % and 6.79 %, respectively. Fig. 3(g) displays the TEM image of Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs, where the block-like structure at the transparent edge corresponds to CoP, and the stacked region corresponds to Fe2P. In Fig. 3(h), the HRTEM image of Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs reveals lattice spacing measurements, where the lattice spacing of 0.22 nm corresponds to the (111) crystal plane of Fe2P [31], the lattice spacing of 0.24 nm corresponds to the (111) crystal plane of CoP [32], and the lattice spacing of 0.28 nm corresponds to the (011) crystal plane of CoP [33]. Figs. 3(i) and (j) show the elemental mapping images, which demonstrate the presence of Co, Fe, P, C, N, and S elements in the composite catalyst. In the case of Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs, the NSCQDs used in the preparation process contains N and S elements, and the elemental mapping results confirm the presence of N and S elements. This indicates the successful doping of N and S elements into CQDs and the successful incorporation of NSCQDs into the composite catalyst, thereby confirming the successful preparation of Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs.

Fig. 4 (a) shows the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of the samples, and (b) presents the pore size distribution curves. The results were obtained using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. In Fig. 4(a), it can be observed that the samples exhibit typical Type IV isotherms with pronounced H3 hysteresis loops, indicating a predominantly mesoporous pore size distribution [34]. The pore size distribution curve in Fig. 4(b) reveals that the pores are mainly distributed within the range of less than 50 nm. The adsorption performance parameters of the catalysts listed in Table 2 indicate that the average pore sizes of Co-P, Fe-P, Co-Fe-P3, and Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs are 15.48, 16.20, 19.56, and 15.46 nm, respectively, confirming the predominantly mesoporous nature of the prepared materials. The specific surface areas of Co-P, Fe-P, Co-Fe-P3, and Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs are 13.74, 10.12, 13.87, and 13.33 m2·g−1, respectively. By comparison, it can be observed that the composite catalyst Co-Fe-P3 exhibits a larger specific surface area than Co-P and Fe-P, providing more active sites for hydrogen evolution, which is beneficial for efficient hydrogen production. The addition of NSCQDs in the composite catalyst Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs slightly reduces the specific surface area, pore volume, and pore size compared to Co-Fe-P3. This is attributed to the presence of NSCQDs. As carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are nanoparticles with sizes less than 10 nm, they can adhere not only to the surface of the material but also within the pores of the porous material, resulting in a slightly smaller specific surface area, pore volume, and pore size compared to Co-Fe-P3. This indirectly indicates the successful incorporation of NSCQDs in the composite material, which can provide more active sites for catalysis, enhance electron transfer, and improve hydrogen evolution activity.

To explore the chemical composition of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and the changes in elements after doping, as well as the chemical composition and interface interactions of each catalyst, XPS analysis was conducted. Fig. 5 shows the XPS spectra of CQDs and each catalyst. Fig. 5(a) presents the full spectrum of CQDs and NSCQDs, revealing the presence of elements O, N, C, and S in CQDs. Fig. 5(b) displays the C 1s spectrum of CQDs and NSCQDs, which can be deconvoluted into four peaks: COOH (288.8 eV), C-S (287.9 eV), CN (285.8 eV), and CC (284.8 eV) [35,36]. Comparatively, the C-S and CN peaks in NSCQDs are stronger than those in CQDs, indicating successful nitrogen-sulfur co-doping in CDQs. To investigate the specific changes in sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N) after doping, the S 2p and N 1s spectra were further analyzed. Fig. 5(c) presents the S 2p spectrum, which can be deconvoluted into peaks corresponding to sulfur sulfate, sulfone, sulfoxide, thiophene, and pyrite, with binding energies ranging from (168.5–169.5) eV, (167.5–168.5) eV, (165.0–166.0) eV, (164.0–164.4) eV, and (163.6–164.0) eV, respectively [37]. Fig. 5(d) shows the N 1s spectrum, which can be deconvoluted into peaks corresponding to pyridinic nitrogen (398.29 eV), pyrrolic nitrogen (400.89 eV), and graphitic nitrogen (402.19 eV) [38–40]. Table 3 provides the relative content of different sulfur and nitrogen species. By comparing the relative content changes of different sulfur species between CQDs and NSCQDs, it is found that the relative content of sulfate sulfur, sulfone, and sulfoxide in high-sulfur coal-based carbon quantum dots prepared by self-doping is higher than that in regular CQDs. The organic sulfur content (sulfone, sulfoxide, thiophene) in NSCQDs accounts for 68.42 %, 57.72 %, which is 10.7 % higher than that in CQDs. The pyridinic nitrogen and pyrrolic nitrogen in NSCQDs account for 23.89 % and 55.68 %, respectively, while in CQDs, they account for 14.57 % and 26.72 %, respectively. The increased proportion of pyridinic nitrogen and pyrrolic nitrogen in NSCQDs indicates the presence of nitrogen doping in the form of pyridinic nitrogen and pyrrolic nitrogen.

Fig. 5(e) shows the overall spectra of the catalysts, revealing the presence of Na, Co, Fe, O, N, C, S, and P elements in the composite catalysts. This provides preliminary evidence for the successful preparation of Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs. Fig. 5(f) displays the Co 2p spectrum of the catalysts, which can be fitted with six characteristic peaks. Peaks at binding energies of 778.4 and 793.7 eV can be attributed to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 of the Co-P bond, respectively [41–43]. Peaks at binding energies of 781.1 and 796.6 eV can be attributed to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 of the Co-O bond, respectively, indicating the formation of Co-O bonds due to surface oxidation of the catalyst. Characteristic satellite peaks are observed at binding energies of 786.0 and 802.1 eV [44,45]. It can be observed from Fig. 5(f) that the characteristic peaks of the composite catalyst Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs appear at slightly higher binding energies compared to the single catalyst Co-P, indicating a shift in the electronic structure. Fig. 5(g) shows the Fe 2p spectrum, which can be fitted with six characteristic peaks. Peaks at binding energies of 707.1 and 719.6 eV can be attributed to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 of the Fe-P bond, respectively. Peaks at binding energies of 711.2 and 723.5 eV can be attributed to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 of the Fe-O bond, respectively [46,47]. Characteristic satellite peaks are observed at binding energies of 715.3 and 734.4 eV [48]. The characteristic peaks of the composite catalyst Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs appear at slightly lower binding energies compared to the single catalyst Fe-P, indicating a shift in the electronic structure.

Fig. 5(h) presents the P 2p spectrum, where the peaks at binding energies of 129.3 and 130.2 eV correspond to P 2p3/2 and P 2p1/2 of the metal phosphide (P-Co or P-Fe) bonds. The bonding mode of P element corresponds to that of Co element and Fe element. The peak at 133.7 eV can be attributed to P-O, which is formed by the oxidation of phosphates in the air [49]. In Fig. 5(h) P 2p spectrum, it can be found that the composite catalyst Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs moves 0.3 eV in the direction of higher binding energy compared with the single catalyst Co-P, compared with the catalyst Fe-P, it moves 0.3 eV in the direction of lower binding energy. In summary, in Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs, the characteristic peaks of Fe 2p and P 2p shift to lower binding energies by 0.3 eV compared to the single catalyst Fe-P, indicating electron transfer between Co-P and Fe-P. This is attributed to the formation of an internal electric field between Co-P and Fe-P, which promotes the transfer of photogenerated electrons from Co-P to Fe-P. The Co 2p and P 2p of the composite catalyst shift to higher binding energies, indicating the formation of an internal electric field [50,51]. This leads to a closely coupled interface between Co-P and Fe-P, resulting in charge transfer. The electron cloud density of the Co, Fe, and P atoms in Co-P and Fe-P changes, suggesting the formation of a heterostructure between Co-P and Fe-P.

To investigate the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance of various catalysts, a series of hydrogen evolution experiments were conducted as shown in Fig. 6 . Fig. 6(a) presents the hydrogen evolution activity test of different catalysts. It can be observed that the hydrogen evolution activity of the elemental catalysts Fe-P and Co-P reaches 13 μmol and 176 μmol, respectively. Compared to the elemental catalysts, the hydrogen evolution activity of the composite catalysts is significantly higher. Among them, the optimal composite catalyst combination is Co-Fe-P3, with a hydrogen evolution activity of 232 μmol. By comparing the amount of hydrogen evolution of each catalyst, the main component of Co-P is CoP, which provides the main active site [52]. The main component of Fe-P is Fe2P, which not only provides the active site, but also plays a structural supporting role. SCQDs is a typical electron transport medium. To further optimize the composite catalyst, a certain proportion of NSCQDs was added to each catalyst, and the hydrogen evolution test is shown in Fig. 6(b). The hydrogen evolution activity of each catalyst is improved to varying degrees after the addition of NSCQDs. The best combination of catalysts is the modified Co-Fe-P3, which achieves a high hydrogen evolution activity of 699 μmol after incorporating 5 % NSCQDs, approximately three times that of Co-Fe-P3. To explore the optimal dosage of NSCQDs, experiments were conducted using different proportions of NSCQDs in the Co-Fe-P3 composite. Fig. 6(c) shows the hydrogen evolution activity test of the catalysts with different proportions of NSCQDs. The results indicate that the hydrogen evolution activity does not increase linearly with the dosage of NSCQDs. When the dosage of NSCQDs is below 5 %, the hydrogen evolution activity gradually increases with an increasing dosage of NSCQDs. However, when the dosage of NSCQDs exceeds 5 %, the hydrogen evolution activity starts to decrease. This is because an excessive dosage of NSCQDs leads to aggregation, covering some active sites and causing pore blockage, resulting in a decrease in hydrogen evolution activity. To verify the significant advantages of NSCQDs as a co-catalyst in improving hydrogen evolution activity, tests were conducted by combining different types of CQDs with Co-Fe-P3, as shown in Fig. 6(d). The hydrogen evolution activity test results of the catalysts with different types of CQDs indicate that Co-Fe-P3/5 %NCQDs and Co-Fe-P3/5 %SCQDs exhibit higher hydrogen evolution activity than Co-Fe-P3/5 %CQDs. The catalyst with the optimal hydrogen evolution activity is Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs, indicating that nitrogen-sulfur co-doped coal-based carbon quantum dots have a significant advantage in photocatalytic hydrogen evolution compared to non-doped coal-based carbon quantum dots. The hydrogen evolution environment is a crucial factor affecting hydrogen evolution activity. To explore the optimal hydrogen evolution environment pH, experiments were conducted under different pH conditions. Fig. 6(e) shows the hydrogen evolution rate test of Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs under different pH conditions. The results indicate that the optimal pH is 10, with the highest hydrogen evolution rate reaching 28.41 mmol·g−1·h−1. This is because in the strong alkali hydrogen production environment, the H+ supply capacity is insufficient, resulting in a decrease in the driving force for the reduction of H+ to H2, which reduces the hydrogen evolution activity. When the system is in an acidic environment, a relatively high concentration of H + will cause the solution to undergo protonation, thereby leading to the insufficient supply of electrons and a decrease in charge utilization efficiency, which is unfavorable for hydrogen evolution [53,54].

Fig. 6(f) shows the hydrogen evolution cycling experiment of Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs, revealing that even after five cycles, the hydrogen production of Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs can still reach 526.23 μmol, indicating the stable hydrogen evolution performance of the newly prepared catalyst. Figure S1. (f) shows the XRD patterns of Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs before and after the reaction. The Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs after 25 h hydrogen evolution cycle was compared with the XRD results of the new samples. It was found that the relative peak position and peak strength had no obvious change, indicating that the sample had good structural stability after 25 h hydrogen evolution cycle. To further investigate the performance and practical application level of the catalyst, AQE testing and STH conversion efficiency analysis of the catalyst Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs were conducted at different wavelengths (420, 475, 520, 550, and 600 nm). Figs. 6(g) and (h) indicate that the composite catalyst Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs exhibits optimal catalytic performance at a wavelength of 520 nm, with an AQE of 4.93 % and an STH conversion efficiency of 0.46 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL spectroscopy and TRPL spectroscopy analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the mechanism of photoinduced electron transfer, steady-state fluorescence experiments and transient fluorescence lifetime decay experiments were conducted on the catalysts. Fig. 7 (a) shows the steady-state fluorescence spectra, from which it can be observed that the EY aqueous solution exhibits a prominent peak at 540 nm, indicating the formation of excited electron-hole pairs. The intensity of fluorescence can reflect the degree of electron-hole recombination. Among the composite catalysts, Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs exhibits the lowest fluorescence intensity, indicating the highest carrier separation efficiency [55]. In the Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs composite catalyst, the formation of Co-P and Fe-P heterojunctions may create conditions for charge transfer and promote electron transfer [56,57]. The addition of NSCQDs provides more reaction sites for carriers [58], synergistically enhancing the separation efficiency of photoinduced electrons and holes in conjunction with Co-P and Fe-P. Therefore, the composite catalyst exhibits the lowest fluorescence peak intensity.

Transient fluorescence lifetime decay experiments were conducted to observe the kinetics of electron transfer [59]. The fluorescence lifetimes of the catalysts Co-P, Fe-P, and NSCQDs show varying degrees of decay compared to EY.

Fig. 7(b) shows the transient fluorescence lifetime plot, and the relevant parameters in Table 4 are obtained from Eqs. (1) and (2). Table 4 presents the fluorescence decay parameters of the samples. Compared to individual Co-P, Fe-P, and NSCQDs, EY exhibits the longest average lifetime (1.35 ns), while the composite catalyst Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs has the shortest average lifetime (0.14 ns), indicating a higher charge separation efficiency and faster transfer rate of photoinduced electrons in Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs. The shortened average lifetime implies faster decay kinetics. The improvement in this performance can be attributed to the introduction of NSCQDs, which acts as both a bridge for electron transfer and a reservoir for electron storage during the electron transfer process. NSCQDs can accelerate charge separation and increase the number of electrons [60]. In the reaction process, the synergistic effect between NSCQDs and Co-P, Fe-P leads to a lower charge recombination rate, collectively promoting the transfer and migration of photoinduced charges.

Fig. 8 presents the results of the photoelectrochemical tests. Fig. 8(a) shows the transient photocurrent response plots of Co-P, Fe-P, NSCQDs, Co-Fe-P3, and Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs. Under intermittent illumination, each sample exhibits distinct photocurrent responses. Compared to Co-P and Fe-P, Co-Fe-P3 demonstrates a higher photocurrent response, indicating that the composite catalyst effectively delays the rapid recombination of photoinduced electrons and holes. The photocurrent response of Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs is stronger than that of Co-Fe-P3, suggesting that NSCQDs enhances electron transfer efficiency and effectively suppresses the rapid recombination of photoinduced electrons and holes, further enhancing photocatalytic activity [61]. Even after five cycles of intermittent illumination, the composite catalyst maintains a relatively stable photocurrent response, demonstrating its excellent photoelectrochemical stability [62].

Fig. 8(b) shows the electrochemical impedance spectra of Co-P, Fe-P, NSCQDs, Co-Fe-P3, and Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs. The magnitude of the curvature radius of the curves reflects the charge transfer resistance. A relatively smaller curvature radius indicates a lower charge transfer resistance and faster charge transfer rate. The curvature radius of Co-Fe-P3 is smaller than that of Co-P and Fe-P, indicating that the combination of Co-P and Fe-P forms a special electron transfer channel, which facilitates enhanced charge transfer rate. Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs exhibits the smallest curvature radius, indicating the lowest electron transfer resistance [63], highest conductivity, and promotes electron transfer, thereby improving catalytic activity [64,65]. This is attributed to the structural advantages of Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs, as the sample surface possesses abundant porous structures, providing more active sites for electron transfer and catalytic reactions. The introduction of NSCQDs enhances the charge transfer rate, thereby improving conductivity and electron-hole pair transfer rate.

Fig. 8(c) depicts the LSV (linear sweep voltammetry) curves of Co-P, Fe-P, NSCQDs, Co-Fe-P3, and Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs electrodes. Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs exhibits the fastest growth of current density and lower overpotential at the same current density, indicating its superiority in photocatalytic hydrogen evolution [66]. The reduced overpotential is mainly due to the irregular nanoscale porous layered structure of Co-Fe-P3 and the superior charge transfer ability resulting from the introduction of NSCQDs, thereby imparting strong hydrogen evolution activity to the Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs composite catalyst.

Fig. 8(d) shows the Zeta potential measurements of Co-MOF, Fe-MOF, and NSCQDs. Before phosphorization, the catalysts were assembled via electrostatic self-assembly. In Fig. 8(d), Co-MOF and Fe-MOF surfaces carry positive charges, while NSCQDs have a negative surface charge, with corresponding Zeta potentials of 1.50 mV, 29.16 mV, and −17.76 mV, respectively. Therefore, Co-MOF and NSCQDs, as well as Fe-MOF and NSCQDs, have opposite Zeta potentials. Through electrostatic attraction, they can come into close contact with each other [67], which also benefits the improvement of system stability, enhancement of charge transfer rate, and photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity.

To investigate the oxidation ability of the catalysts, hydroxyl radical trapping experiments were conducted on each catalyst. Based on the fact that the reaction between terephthalic acid and hydroxyl radicals generates fluorescent hydroxy terephthalic acid, terephthalic acid was chosen as a hydroxyl radical scavenger in this study. By performing steady-state fluorescence measurements and observing the changes in fluorescence intensity over different time periods, the quantity of hydroxyl radicals generated during the reaction process can be indirectly determined [68–71]. Photogenerated holes generated at the VB (valence band) of the photocatalyst can oxidize OH−and H2O, leading to the formation of hydroxyl radicals [72]. The greater the quantity of hydroxyl radicals, the stronger the fluorescence intensity, indicating a higher hole oxidation ability of the catalyst. Fig. 9 shows the fluorescence intensity at different time intervals after the addition of terephthalic acid alkaline solution to each sample. From Fig. 9(a), it can be observed that Co-P and Co-Fe-P exhibit an increase in fluorescence intensity with prolonged illumination time, indicating the accumulation of light energy within the reaction system. The fluorescence intensity is positively correlated with the generation of hydroxyl radicals in the system [73,74], indicating the formation of more photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Fig. 9(b) depicts the fluorescence intensity at different time intervals for Co-Fe-P and Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs. With increasing illumination time, the fluorescence intensity gradually increases, and the system containing NSCQDs exhibits stronger fluorescence intensity. In the presence of NSCQDs, more photogenerated electron-hole pairs are generated in the system, indicating a stronger oxidation ability in the Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs system.

Figs. 10 (a) and (b) show the Mott-Schottky measurement results of Co-P and Fe-P. The type of semiconductor can be determined based on the positive or negative slope of the curves. In Figs. 10(a) and (b), the slopes of Co-P and Fe-P are positive, indicating n-type semiconductors. The flat band potentials (Efb) of Co-P and Fe-P are −0.48 V vs. SCE and −0.55 V vs. SCE, respectively. From Figs. 10(a) and (b), it can be determined that the curves have a positive slope, indicating that Co-P and Fe-P are n-type semiconductors [75]. For n-type semiconductors, the conduction band potential (ECB) is typically about 0.1–0.2 V more negative than the flat band potential (Efb) [76]. Therefore, the ECB values of Co-P and Fe-P are approximately −0.68 V vs. SCE and −0.75 V vs. SCE, respectively, which correspond to −0.44 V vs. NHE and −0.51 V vs. NHE.

Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) was used to analyze the band structure. Figs. 10(c) and (d) show the UPS of Co-P and Fe-P, respectively. The measured UPS widths of Co-P and Fe-P are 15.47 eV and 15.61 eV, respectively. The valence band maximum (EVB) relative to vacuum is obtained by subtracting the excitation energy (21.22 eV) [77]. The EVB values of Co-P and Fe-P are 5.75 eV and 5.61 eV, respectively. According to the relationship between the standard hydrogen electrode potential (Ee) and the absolute energy level (Eabs) (Ee = Eabs - 4.44) [78], the EVB values of Co-P and Fe-P are determined to be 1.31 V vs. NHE and 1.17 V vs. NHE, respectively.

Fig. 11 depicts the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution mechanism of the Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs system. Based on the hydroxyl radical trapping experiments, it is known that the Co-Fe-P3/5 %CQDs system exhibits the strongest oxidation ability. Furthermore, since Fe-P has a more negative ECB, indicating a stronger reduction capability, and Co-P has a more positive EVB, indicating a stronger oxidation ability, it can be inferred that the Co-Fe-P3/5 %CQDs system exhibits charge transfer in the S-type heterojunction structure. Due to the lower valence band of Co-P compared to Fe-P, the photo-generated holes of Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs are located on Co-P [79,80]. Based on these results, it can be speculated that the direction of charge transfer conforms to the charge transfer mechanism in an S-type heterojunction structure. Due to the staggered band structure of Co-P and Fe-P shown in Fig. 11, it can be inferred from the bandgap analysis of Co-P and Fe-P that their band structures interlace with each other, with both Co-P and Fe-P exhibiting n-type semiconductor characteristics. Therefore, it is suggested that Co-P and Fe-P form an S-type heterojunction structure [81,82]. Due to the staggered band structures of Co-P and Fe-P, with the assistance of NSCQDs, the charge density between the catalysts can be increased [83]. Electrons on the conduction band of Co-P spontaneously transfer to the valence band of Fe-P, thereby establishing an internal electric field between Co-P, Fe-P, and NSCQDs. The Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs material is a nano-porous material that is advantageous for dye molecule adsorption and greatly enhances the efficiency of the reaction. Dye molecules adsorbed on the surface of Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs, when excited by light, will be converted to singlet excited state EY1*, which further converts to EY3*. At this stage, in the presence of TEOA, EY3* is transformed into the strongly reducible EY− while TEOA is reduced to TEOA+ [84]. With the assistance of NSCQDS, EY− transfers electrons to the surface of Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs, resulting in hydrogen evolution. The high efficiency and smooth progress of the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction are attributed, on one hand, to the nano-porous structure of Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs, which allows dye molecules to adsorb on the material surface and shuttle freely within the material's pores, providing more reaction sites. On the other hand, NSCQDs acts as a bridge for efficient electron transfer and can serve as a temporary electron reservoir during the reaction. These factors collectively contribute to the highly efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution.

High-sulfur coal was used as the carbon source, and urea was used as the nitrogen source to successfully and greenly synthesize NSCQDs via a hydrothermal method. The obtained NSCQDs was incorporated into Co-Fe-P composite materials for photocatalytic water splitting and hydrogen evolution. Compared to Co-P and Fe-P, the Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs composite exhibited the highest hydrogen evolution rate of 28.41 mmol·g−1·h−1 at pH 10. At a wavelength of 520 nm, the AQE reached 4.93%, and the STH conversion efficiency reached 0.46%. The improvement in the photocatalytic performance of the composite material can be attributed to the following factors. The nano-porous structure of Co-Fe-P3/5 %NSCQDs provides abundant mesoporous structures, offering numerous active sites. NSCQDs acts as a bridge for electron transfer and can also serve as a temporary electron reservoir, increasing the charge density between the catalysts, enhancing electron transfer efficiency, and improving conductivity and electron-hole pair transfer rates. This effectively suppresses the rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. The S-type heterojunction structure also contributes to efficient electron transfer.

Shiyong Xu and Mei Li conceived and designed experiments; Shiyong Xu and Yue Wu conducted experiments; Mei Li contributed reagents/materials and analytical tools, Yijun Wang assisted in data analysis and processing, Shiyong Xu and Mei Li jointly wrote the first draft.
